en.extract:
The minute hermit (Phaethornis idaliae) is a tiny species of hummingbird in the family Trochilidae. It is endemic to Brazil.


Taxonomy and systematics
The minute hermit was for a time considered conspecific with the little hermit (P. longuemareus) and now is treated with it as a superspecies. It is monotypic.


Description
The minute hermit is one of the world's smallest birds. It is 8.4 to 9.2 cm (3.31 to 3.62 in) long and weighs 1.8 to 2.7 g (0.06 to 0.10 oz). The male has dark iridescent green upperparts and a dark brown throat. The female's underparts are reddish orange. Both sexes have a black "mask" and a pale supercilium and malar stripe.


Distribution and habitat
The minute hermit is found in southeastern Brazil from approximately Bahia south to Rio de Janeiro state. It inhabits the understory of primary or lightly disturbed forest including their edges, and also mature secondary forest and forested islands. It has been recorded in Eucalyptus plantations near its preferred habitat. In elevation it ranges from sea level to 500 m (1,640 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The minute hermit is assumed to be sedentary.


Feeding
The minute hermit is a "trap-line" feeder like other hermit hummingbirds, visiting a circuit of flowering plants for nectar. It also consumes small arthropods.


Breeding
The minute hermit's breeding season spans from October to February. Males display to females at leks. Its nest is an open cup suspended from the underside of a drooping leaf. The clutch of two eggs is incubated solely by the female.


Vocalization
The minute hermit's song is "a high-pitched note repeated 3–4 times, followed by an accelerated descending warble" sung from a low perch.


Status
The IUCN has assessed the minute hermit as being of Least Concern, though its population size is unknown and believed to be decreasing. It has a very restricted range in the Atlantic Forest, a habitat that has almost entirely been deforested. It does occur in a few protected areas.


References

The '''minute hermit''' ('''''Phaethornis idaliae''''') is a tiny [[species]] of [[hummingbird]] in the family [[Trochilidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Brazil]].<ref name=IOC11.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/ |title=IOC World Bird List (v 11.2) |last1=Gill |first1= F. |last2=Donsker|first2=D.|last3=Rasmussen |first3=P. |date=July 2021 |access-date=July 14, 2021 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The minute hermit was for a time considered [[conspecific]] with the [[little hermit]] (''P. longuemareus'') and now is treated with it as a [[superspecies]].<ref name=SACClist>{{cite web|author=Remsen, J. V., Jr.|author2= J. I. Areta|author3= E. Bonaccorso|author4= S. Claramunt|author5= A. Jaramillo|author6= D. F. Lane|author7= J. F. Pacheco|author8= M. B. Robbins|author9= F. G. Stiles|author10= K. J. Zimmer|date=24 August 2021|title=A classification of the bird species of South America|publisher=American Ornithological Society |url=https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline03.htm|access-date=24 August 2021}}</ref> It is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC11.2/>

==Description==

The minute hermit is one of the world's smallest birds.<ref>{{cite book|last=Wood|first=Gerald L. |title=The Guinness Book of Animal Facts and Feats|publisher=Sterling Pub Co Inc. |year=1983|isbn=978-0-85112-235-9}}</ref> It is {{cvt|8.4|to|9.2|cm|in|2}} long and weighs {{cvt|1.8|to|2.7|g|oz|2}}. The male has dark iridescent green upperparts and a dark brown throat. The female's underparts are reddish orange. Both sexes have a black "mask" and a pale [[supercilium]] and [[malar stripe]].<ref name=MIHE-BOW>{{cite book |author=Hinkelmann, C.|author2= G. M. Kirwan|author3=P. F. D. Boesman |year=2020|chapter=Minute Hermit (''Phaethornis idaliae'')|version=1.0|title=Birds of the World |editor=J. del Hoyo|editor2= A. Elliott |editor3= J. Sargatal|editor4= D. A. Christie|editor5=E. de Juana|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|location=Ithaca, NY, USA|doi=10.2173/bow.minher1.01}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The minute hermit is found in southeastern Brazil from approximately [[Bahia]] south to [[Rio de Janeiro (state)|Rio de Janeiro]] state. It inhabits the understory of [[primary forest|primary]] or lightly disturbed forest including their edges, and also mature [[secondary forest]] and forested islands. It has been recorded in ''Eucalyptus'' plantations near its preferred habitat. In elevation it ranges from sea level to {{cvt|500|m|ft|-1}}.<ref name=MIHE-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The minute hermit is assumed to be sedentary.<ref name=MIHE-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The minute hermit is a "[[Trap-lining|trap-line]]" feeder like other hermit hummingbirds, visiting a circuit of flowering plants for nectar. It also consumes small [[arthropod]]s.<ref name=MIHE-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The minute hermit's breeding season spans from October to February. Males display to females at [[Lek mating|leks]]. Its nest is an open cup suspended from the underside of a drooping leaf. The clutch of two eggs is incubated solely by the female.<ref name=MIHE-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Phaethornis-idaliae |species=minute hermit}}
===Vocalization===

The minute hermit's song is "a high-pitched note repeated 3–4 times, followed by an accelerated descending warble" sung from a low perch.<ref name=MIHE-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the minute hermit as being of Least Concern, though its population size is unknown and believed to be decreasing.<ref name=IUCN/> It has a very restricted range in the [[Atlantic Forest]], a habitat that has almost entirely been deforested. It does occur in a few protected areas.<ref name=MIHE-BOW/>

zh.extract:
姬隐蜂鸟（台湾作微隱蜂鳥，学名：Phaethornis idaliae），是雨燕目蜂鸟科的一种鸟类。
姬隐蜂鸟体重约2.6克，翼长约34.4毫米，嘴峰长约23.1毫米，喙宽度约1.7毫米，喙厚度约1.8毫米，跗蹠长约3.8毫米，尾长约26.8毫米。姬隐蜂鸟在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在密集的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是植物的花蜜。
姬隐蜂鸟分布于巴西东南部的低地（巴伊亚至里约热内卢）。该物种是单型物种，没有亚种分化。
